speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the family-- my, my son was bar mitzvahed in Culver City in Temple Akiba. He learned good. He's got his bachelor degree. And what happened-- he had to do a resume after he came out of college, Northridge, for tax assessor. He was a real American at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was good. They took him right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: we go back to your new family, certainly-- your, your son and grandson, daughter-in-law, let's go back-- we didn't discuss about-- in the camp, you were given a number. Talk about that experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: The moment we came in, we got a number. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [POINTS] That's the moment we came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: To which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Birkenau. That's where I got the number. Not in Auschwitz, in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that moment of when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it hurt a little bit. And the one who was giving us a number was a prisoner himself who was tattooed, too. He lives in Florida. I think he's still living, I don't know. He's a friend of Max Aldewereld. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went on the number. Then I was put in the barracks. That's when I, next day, had to go shoveling, 5 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the luck-- well, I-- it was tragic that the transports came in. Through-- a barber, being a barber, it saved me. And that's how it went.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the first few months was terrible-- every morning, taking the barrels out to empty them. We're standing-- somebody stole my shoes. I was on bare feet. And then I stole from somebody else. That's how it was there then. Everybody was stealing from one another if they could. There was no, no friendship. I don't know what it was like. I don't know what it was. And-- because they knew they were not going to make it anymore. And that was the-- I could say that I became a barber in the [GERMAN]. That was-- that saved me-- a part of my life. But it was tragic to cut the women. They were crying, beautiful women from Hungary and from-- men from, also, Hungary. There was a part who spoke no Yiddish, from Hungary, and a part who spoke Yiddish. In Hungary, there are two parts who speak Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they asked me every time, what's going to happen? I said, you'll have to have to work. Because the SS says, don't say anything what you see. And we were not allowed. They stand behind us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The beautiful hair-- they were taken away, the hair, in sacks and taken away. And they were given uniforms. And they were taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you didn't allow friendships too much. But you made a friend of Max.

speaker: interviewee
text: Max, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that friendship develop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was-- because I was Dutch. We were-- we were not many Dutch-- funny. And we became friends in Auschwitz. And he always brought us something.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in the-- what do they call that? Canada, they called it. That's where he was. If he's interviewed, you will hear everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Believe me, he helped us a lot with the food. And he cooked with us on that. We stayed alive with-- looking out that nothing happened. And thank god it didn't-- till the end of the crematorium, till we were transported, and the big walk to Gleiwitz. In Gleiwitz, I missed him. And after the war, I saw him back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you trusted each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then one friend I had-- Wimpy Pollock, who was in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sosnowitz-- in that camp. And they came back when I had stolen out of the kitchen. And then when he-- he came to me in the hospital in Amsterdam. I can't find the letter-- what he wrote was unbelievable. Jack, if it wasn't for you, we would have been dead. What you had done for us-- the food what you put away under our blankets, it's-- no diamond, no gold can pay what you tried to do for us. He lives in Montreal. Perhaps I get ahold of him-- Wimpy. I left them after it. It was 35 years ago, I saw him the last time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any talk about escaping with the other inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We saw people escape, but they were caught. And they were shot in front of us, sitting in a chair, through the head. And then, one day, we-- all the barracks at Birkenau were empty-- had to be empty. We all had to come-- hundreds of us-- stand. And two Polish-- one was Jewish, the other was Gentile-- they were caught in their barracks with radios. And the next day they were taken out by the SS-- a big platform, there, the SS general standing there. They told them what they'd done. And, in front of us, they were going to be hanged. And they put them the chair with the rope around them. We saw them. And then they said, long live-- in Polish, and one in Yiddish-- Poland. And they were dead. When that was finished, we could go back to-- back to our barracks. The bodies what I saw was thousands-- thousands and thousands. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At the end-- it was something-- you didn't think about it anymore. You walked over them. That was in Flossenburg and in Sachsenhausen. Because they didn't have the crematorium. I don't know if they had the crematoriums there. But the-- Birkenau, they went right to the gas chamber. The sick people went right to the-- and the-- and the old women, and the mothers, and the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any mention of religion? Did you ever talk of religion inside the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard a story that very religious Polish people were still saying with the books going right in the gas chamber. That's what we heard. But further, the Polish people were still there. You know, they-- we always made a joke-- now we're going to get matzohs. We never had them, of course. But never, never--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't say prayers together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it every night. I do it every night-- never said prayers together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all so afraid they're coming. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you get news from one barracks to another? Or did you just stay within the confines of the people--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I stayed in my confine, where I was. And then, the next day, we got up. And I walked. We couldn't talk. If we talked, they says-- in German they scream, be quiet-- with the SS with the dogs, walk into the place where we were grieving. And with that [? 'Alexander ?] [? Ragtime'-- ?] but it's-- I got that as barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why-- the people say, how did you get out? I don't know. It was God on my side. I don't know. Like angels they came in, those two military men who took me to another hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometimes, when I'm in bed, I kind of-- and then I get-- I see the memory of my wife and children calling me, when I was pulled out by the SS. And that's when I wrote the article in the why? Why? Why did they kill them? Why? What did they do? They didn't do anything. They were children playing with other children, and nothing. There was no hate. Later, yeah, when the Germans came in, a lot of Dutch people changed. There was one family, 21, all saved. They were hiding in the brewery von Heinekens. You know, the beer? Hiding-- the whole family was saved for two years, underneath.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people were saved. Some, not many. Holland had about around 187,000 people. After the war, about 6,000-- Sephardic, only 60. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't touch the Sephardic temple. They did nothing to it. But the Ashkenaz shuls were all damaged. It was saved by the Spanish and Portuguese consul-- ambassador, they said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember giving yourself any messages about surviving-- of wanting to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later-- I didn't want to survive, when I was in the hospital because I knew everybody was dead. But the nuns-- I could say the nuns talked me out of it. You know, in the article with me is-- Bill Conruh talked about the nuns. They were so good. They were hiding themselves till the war was over. There were some good people. But the pope, the Catholic pope that time, that was a bad man, believe me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were on that two-day walk in the snow, did one try to help another?

speaker: interviewee
text: We held one another. And then they dropped dead. Then I had-- what's his name? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hoonjie, we called him Hoonjie. His name was de Hoonden. He lives in Amsterdam. I say, try to keep-- I missed him. He met his brother in Gleiwitz. I met him after the war. He's saved. And his brother, he saw-- but he he didn't come back anymore. His brother died in Gleiwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There's still families, you know-- that's what I got a, a letter from Washington. There's a woman. Her name is Pinto. She said she has an uncle, Jack Pinto. I'm trying to find out who that is. It's in the book that I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any Red Cross personnel come into the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or any other visitors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. There was only SS. That's all. Birkenaus was SS, that's all. We saw farmers coming in with a truck with potatoes or that to bring to the kitchen, with some margarine.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember I had to go to the kitchen to pick up something for the [GERMAN], where we was. And I'm there. And one of the prisoners said to me, he says, you want some margarine? I says, OK. So I put it in my-- in my truck, where I was walking. And all of a sudden, he called me back. No, don't do it. Because they check on every-- thank god I didn't take it with me because I would have been shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But seeing those two people shot through the head-- that was-- ooh! [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they're not changed. The German people are not changed. I don't care what they say. They are still the same. They are not changed. I've told the Wiesenthal Center, they are not changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about names of people of, of, of the SS? Did they ever go by names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Wagner. He was a terrible guy. [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]-- other names? Who was head of-- Kramer. He was the head of Birkenau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Other names? I don't remember anymore. It's so many years ago. The memory goes back. OK, let's go back now. You're in the hospital, in recovery, in Amsterdam. And now you've gone and you've met your, your new wife. Share how your, your relationship together--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she had no family anymore. Nine at home-- uncles and aunts. She was the only survivor. It's in the 51 together, aunts, uncles, and all. She's the only survivor. Nine at home. And her father was well-known in Amsterdam, in the textile. He had textile shops in Amsterdam. And she was brought up in, like, in the middle class. And when she came back, there was no family anymore left. And that's how we got together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my brother was living in Amsterdam, in Tilanusstraat. And he had a room there over. And he said, why don't you get the license-- for your father and the tobacco? Perhaps you-- it was all clandestine at that time. You couldn't get it. So I got a license. And in a little place near house, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I began-- I could make cigars. I had two people working, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] making cigars. I was allowed on the quota what I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one night I'm sleeping, there, alone, because two [NON-ENGLISH] came through the window. I saw it. I had a block on the cigar. And I threw it right at them. The police were behind them-- caught them right away. They got one and a half years. Now I'm going to tell you something you won't believe. I have it here. We were going to get married. And in that house, we cleaned it. That's where we did tobacco then. We tore all the wallpaper. In Holland, everybody has wallpaper. We took the wallpaper away. There was also, behind the wallpaper, wood. We took it away. Let me get it for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps it's hanging there-- you can see--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll see it. This is made from Gobelin, 225 years ago. The Wailing Wall in England-- you'll see-- in, uh-- is-- was, was Palestine at the time. And we found it. Anything what you found that you think belongs to a family, you had to give it to the Jewish organization after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: The law is, one year and six months, nobody claims it, it goes to the person who found it. Nobody ever claimed it. And I got it. You see it hanging there? The Wailing Wall? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Made from Gobelin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been offered, in Canada, $10,000 for it. I'm not giving it away. It's going to my grandson. You see them praying-- woman praying in that time for the Wailing Wall, with her old fashions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family hide any possessions before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father hide-- he had one-- my brother told me-- one pail with all Dutch money, quarters, dollars. And he gave it to a milkman to save if he comes back. It was our milkman what came at home, bottles on the-- and that was saved. He was honest. And he had a suit, a jacket, from my father, what my father sewed in a diamond ring, necklace, and a pin. And that's the only thing that came back with the diamonds in it. My sister has it. My brother and I have it. That was the only thing. And to share that money-- gave me that money-- half of it with the [? center. ?] And that was the only thing what we got back. My-- came back, after, I was walking in Amsterdam. I see somebody walking with the carriage with a little baby in it. That was my carriage, from my son. He took-- I heard he took-- I went to him, I said, well, we took all the china and everything. But we had nothing to eat, so I had to sell it. They took it all out of the house. You could do nothing about it, you know, at that time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the rest of the non-Jewish neighbors or friends? Did you see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few after the war who I knew. They had a-- in February, that's where the statue is, in Amsterdam, before the synagogue-- the Spanish-Portuguese synagogue. From the uproar, like a revolution against the Germans, when they start taking the Jews away, they were called-- those Dutch people, they are called Jordaners-- the real Dutch-- old-- in the Jordaan. They have-- they speak a normal Dutch, with a little Jordaan accent, you know, like-- they were good. And they tried to help the Jews. And they had a fight with them. But it was too small. But after the war, they made a big statue of the uprising in February, in 1941, in Amsterdam. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened in Amsterdam, when I-- when I went to the shul, I have-- we still have our place there, going back to 1530-- our own place, in Amsterdam, in the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue-- belongs to our family. We opened the door there. I found half the books here, all of them gold outside-- Jesserun de Pinto. And there, I went in shul every Saturday on the same place and sat there. And who comes walking in but my father-in-law-- from my first wife. He was interned. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the war broke out, the British consul went to his place, where he was making shoes. And they said, Ziff, you've got to come with. We're moving away. All the English people are moving out of here. Take the family. He didn't want to go. He said, my business-- well, he should have gone. And he didn't want to. And then, after that, he was captured by the Germans. He was interned. He went to Vittel, in France. That's where they-- he survived to the intern camp with his second wife. And he came in the shul. He heard I was living. So he came. He was living in Scotland, but he came all the way here. And he said he's coming back next week. He commits suicide in Scotland, in Glasgow, because he saw that his grandchildren and his daughter was dead. He had one son who was a well-known saxophonist. He had a contract to play in-- play in England, but the war broke out and he couldn't come. He died in the intern camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't torture him in the intern camp. He died of a sickness. I can tell you what the sickness was. He was a guy who went out with all  the women-- syphilis. He died. He was-- Max, he, he was about 22. But he thought he still a daughter living and that. So when he went back to Glasgow, he committed suicide in a park in Glasgow. His wife lived. She, she just died. She lived in Amsterdam-- his second wife. The first wife, she died when my daughter was-- my wife was 12 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I didn't know her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the end of my father-in-law. I never saw him again. Then I heard he, he commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue with your, your, your new marriage. Tell about how you got your life back together again and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she got back a license to start the textile again. So we went in the south of Amsterdam, an empty shop-- it was hard to get-- on the corner, Columbus Street. And the name of her father's shops was the Blauwe Winkels-- the Blue Shops. It was well known in Amsterdam. And we opened the shop. I have a photo with my little baby, Daniel, standing in front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in the textile business. So we got textile. When we opened it, 200 people standing outside there because, you know, there was all-- couldn't get no shirts, that time, at all. They bought a lot-- emptied.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got a-- we had that shop for about 12 years. And then all our friends were moving away to America and Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, Dora, the Russians are coming. They're going in-- they're going to Hungary. And we were afraid, so afraid. Do you think we can go away? I said, let's go to America. And we got in contact with our friends in America. So we went to The Hague, to the American consul. I was examined. I was still on the quota. I was not admitted. I had a, a spot on my lung.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife was OK. I said to the doctor, Doctor, this is from malnutrition from the concentration camp. He wouldn't let me go. Well, Wimpy Pollock was living in Canada, Montreal. I wrote him. And I phoned him. He said, come here. You'll get-- you're on the quota also for Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the Canadian consul. They examined me. Everything was-- in two weeks, I had my visa to go to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She couldn't take it-- one and a half years. She worked with the Jewish YMCA in Montreal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we lived in Saint Urbain Street. We were near the mountain there, Mount Montreal. We lived near there. And she worked there, but she couldn't take the cold. It was terrible-- the winter, you know. She had to put men's trousers on. She said, let's try to get to America again. I said, well, let's go to the American consul again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to the consul. I tell them what happened. They said well, we'll examine you. They examined me. They said, you're healthy. Who is that nut in The Hague who said you couldn't go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked and said-- he thanked god. You're four weeks in time for your quota. You still can go to-- in three weeks, I got my visa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- everything what we had in luggage, we sent to California, to our friends. We're coming. And we went with the Greyhound bus. We took a three-and-a-half week tour all the way around America. It was cheap, that time, you know-- time in '59. And September, beginning of October '59, we arrived downtown with the Greyhound bus. And there, the Dutch people are standing there. Why, friends, you are new. Then we went to Culver City. I was in the house by the-- who lives now in Vegas, there, for two weeks. Then we got a house there, near him. And we rented the house-- $85 a week, that time. Now it's all changed. And then I was trying to work. Now, to get a barber license in California-- believe me, you've got to read the whole book. What vein goes, where you laugh-- all that kind of stuff is in there-- and about the head and alopecia, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm studying that book so I could get a license. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was very, very hard to get a license. So I applied to United Airlines. I was accepted because I spoke German and English and some little French and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I read-- that was through the I read the on a Saturday morning. We need a barber in the base-- not merely a base where the rockets are made-- not rockets, space station. You don't need a license. But you're not allowed to help civilians, only military people. I said, well, let me go there. That was on Arbor Vitae.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come on Saturday morning. I talk with the boss, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Rick Kell, originally from Oregon-- he had asthma, terrible. I told him-- he said, do you want to start Monday? I said OK. And that's all how it started.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a barber there. His name was Dietrich. I had a white coat. And he could never saw my number. He said, don't go by here-- by the cleaner here. He's a Jew. That was my experience. He was a-- so I said, I put up my money. He said, I don't like him. The other Jewish people-- I have friends-- all of a sudden, he has friends. His name was Dietrich. And he left. My boss got very sick. I, I was there in the shop for two years. And the general-- Rick Kell has to give it up. He's very sick. His wife is keeping up the concession.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she can't keep-- she wants to go back to Oregon. Would you like to have the concession? But you've got to ride in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me-- full colonel, van de Meer. But he was-- he was American-- Dutch name and ancestors. He said, Jack, I got the concession. He said, if you don't have the money to build in it-- we're moving from Arbor Vitae to El Segundo. And there I got the concession to open the new barber shop in El Segundo. He said, if you don't have the money, I'll lend it to you. You don't have to pay no interest. Thank god we took money from our shop what we had in, in Holland. We had about $15,000 when we came here. At that time-- it was a lot of money at that time. And we saved it. And I used it-- I opened the barber shop for $12,000. And this-- the mirror what you see here, the back-- the gold one-- is one of the mirrors from my barber shop. It was opened with a ribbon by the general of the United States. I was-- they loved me there because I was the-- I have telegrams from generals. I had to go to Washington. And one ride, I'm on my way back to California. And on the plane is a movie-- And I see a lot of the-- I see what you went to. He sent me-- I was in the hospital with a back operation. I had some trouble with my back when I came out of the camp. I never talked about it. I had a laminectomy in Kaiser. It came out beautiful. But he sent me a telegram-- four generals. I have it all here, the telegrams from the generals, wishing that I'd come and cut their hair again. And then I went back to the-- and I had it for nearly five years, that shop-- a little longer. And I thought, Jack, it's time. We had 800 customers a week, 800. Doris-- she helped me out sometimes on the cash. And my barbers-- I have the photo of all the barbers. And I gave the concession over. And I went to [? DCast. ?] I still have the badge. You can't come in. You've got to have the badge. And these guys-- space system the reason. I had a little barber shop there. I didn't have to pay rent. And they moved away to Redondo Beach. I didn't want to go. And so I gave it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had friends living here in the valley. And I said, shall we move to the valley? The rent was $400 at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we moved. I could afford it, that time. We saved. And that's how our life started in the valley. And when was your son Maurice born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maurice was born in 1938, two years after we were married-- in 1938. He went to Rosh Pina, the Jewish school in Amsterdam. Rosh Pina, where they learn English.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1948? Maurice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was born after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1948-- maybe-- yeah, '48. And he went to Rosh Pina. That's a Jewish Hebrew school where he went, with a bus that picked him up until-- till we moved to Canada. There, he had to learn French and English, you know-- Quebec. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was OK doing his school. But he-- then we went back-- after that, we came to America. He went in the school. He was put from the third class to nearly the fifth. He was so far in learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here they were-- the learning here was much more backwards than in, in, in Europe, you know? He spoke his English good, a little French. What effect do you think your experiences have had on your raising of Maurice, your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's Jewish, absolute Jewish. He was bar mitzvahed here. My grandson's going to be bar mitzvahed May-- in May, he is going to be bar mitzvahed. He's a member of [HEBREW] shul. That's a conservative--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Daniel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My son is a member of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's important that they be Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And my daughter-in-law is also Sephardic. Her parents came from the Isle of Rhodes, from Greece. She speaks Ladino, my daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had a very big, Sephardic wedding. Her family-- all her family's here, you know. Well-known-- her family's well-known. One was-- has a bank, and that and that. And we had a very big, Sephardic wedding. When she cooks her-- Pesach is with grape leaves, you know? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland, the Sephardic were different than those from Morocco or from Greece. They all speak Ladino. In Holland, nobody spoke Ladino. They spoke the British-- the Dutch language. Only the rabbi-- the rabbi received a photo of my-- when I'm being the chuppah-- the 'chuppah,' we call that-- is dressed like Napoleon. The rabbi from-- he's walking in front of me, bringing me into the shul. And we called the shul the [NON-ENGLISH], all Sephardic names. When it's-- when it's Sukkot, the Sukkot we call 'Cabana,' still the Spanish name that, that-- but the history for my family, what I have going back, 1492, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Rabbi Daniel Pinto was a rabbi in New York in 1700. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They came from--
